topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The clearing was an empty space where a towering oak once stood. I walked toward the stump, my boots crunching on dry leaves, and the air felt suddenly thin. It was the same spot. I remembered the sheer, unadulterated pride I’d felt when I first tucked that tiny sapling into the earth when I was eight. Now, there was just a ragged scar on the ground, a monument to some careless, swift violence. A low, tight knot formed in my chest, a pressure that made my breath catch in my throat. I stared at the exposed roots, tracing the lines where the trunk had been, feeling a cold, sharp resentment bloom beneath my ribs. Someone had taken it. Someone had erased that small, hopeful act with a single, brutal sweep of a saw.

[story 2]

"Well, look at that," I muttered, the sound scraping against the silence of the neglected yard. My hands clenched into fists, the knuckles turning white against my palms. The ground felt spongy where the roots should have been, disturbed and raw. I knelt down, pushing aside the debris, and there it was—only the base remained, splintered and pathetic. A wave of profound disappointment washed over me, heavy and suffocating. I remembered the meticulous care I’d taken, the endless watering, the whispered promises to that little green thing. Now, only wreckage. I stood up slowly, shaking my head, a harsh, humorless laugh escaping my lips. It was a foolish investment, a waste of effort, and the injustice of it settled deep, making my jaw ache with unspoken fury.

[story 3]

The afternoon sun cast long shadows across the barren patch of soil. I approached the cut, my gait stiff, every step deliberate and heavy. My gaze swept over the devastation—the missing bulk, the exposed wood—and a strange hollowness echoed inside me. It wasn't just a tree; it was a memory, a tangible piece of a time when things felt manageable, when effort yielded tangible results. A slow, seething resentment began to coil in my stomach. I ran a hand over the rough bark of the remaining stump, the texture abrasive against my skin. I didn't need to shout; the sheer weight of what had been destroyed spoke volumes. I turned away abruptly, the sound of my own heavy breathing the only noise in the quiet expanse, feeling utterly depleted by the sheer unfairness of the scene.
